request.question: What is the second project of the vocalist of the band The Distillers?

title: FOCSA Building
Dol Theeta
Accidents Never Happen
The Distillers
Hristo Botev Stadium (Vratsa)
The ROADEX Project
VentureCrowd
Brody Dalle
Virginia HOT lanes
Catherine Hickson
sentences: In an era when concrete construction of buildings more than 18 storeys tall was considered unaffordable, the FOCSA was only the second project of its kind in the world.
The project was managed by engineer, Luis Sáenz Duplace; the architect was Ernesto Gómez Sampera (1921–2004), who later moved to Puerto Rico where he worked until his death in 2004.
Dol Theeta is a progressive metal band formed in 2008 by Greek music composer Thanasis Lightbridge.
The band represents the second project of Lightbridge's "Electronica Art Metal" vision, the first project being Dol Ammad a more powerful and epic progressive project.
Dol Theeta has a more atmospheric and psychedelic character and combines diverse musical styles such as heavy metal, electronica and opera.
Accidents Never Happen (ANH) was a rock band from Oslo, Norway, formed in 2005.

After releasing their debut album "Oslo Beat" in 2006, vocalist and main songwriter Magne Mostue, guitarist Martin Natvig, drummer Christian Løvhaug and guitarist Jesper Jørgensen moved to Liverpool, UK.
The band's music was influenced significantly by their two-year stay in the UK.

Their second project, "Accidents Never Happen", was recorded in The Motor Museum and St Brides church; (The band's rehearsal space) with Simon Cullwick (who allegedly turned down an offer to work on Razorlight's "Slipway Fires").
"Accidents Never Happen" was a much darker album than "Oslo Beat".
The Distillers were an Australian-American punk rock band, formed in Los Angeles, California in 1998 by vocalist and guitarist Brody Dalle.
Dalle co-wrote, played guitar and provided vocals for nearly every track on the band's three albums.
After the breakup of the band in 2006, Dalle and Distillers guitarist Tony Bevilacqua went on to form Spinnerette.
Hristo Botev Stadium is a multi-purpose stadium in Vratsa, Bulgaria.
It is currently used mostly for football matches.
It has been the home of Botev Vratsa for 60 years.
The stadium was built in 1948 and has a seating capacity of 8,000 spectators.
After the approval of a project worth € 150 000, in 2008 the stadium was renovated and had 3,000 seats, spread in the 3 central sectors.
In June 2009, the Botev Vratsa management approved a second project, worth € 120 000 for reconstruction of sector "V", opposite the central building of the stadium.
The second renovation was completed in 2009.

The ROADEX Project is a technical co-operation between roads organisations across northern Europe part funded by the EU Northern Periphery Programme that aims to share roads related information and research between the partners.

The Project was started in 1998 as a 3 year pilot co-operation between the roads districts of Finnish Lapland, Troms County of Norway, the Northern Region of Sweden and the Highland Council of Scotland and was subsequently followed and extended by a second project, ROADEX II, from 2002 to 2005, a third project, ROADEX III, from 2006 to 2007, and a final project, “ROADEX Network Implementing Accessibility”, from 2009 to 2012.
VentureCrowd is an Australian multi asset class crowdfunding platform, headquartered in Sydney, including equity crowdfunding, property crowdfunding and debt-based crowdfunding.
VentureCrowd completed the largest Australian equity crowdfunding raise, $4.2 million, for taxi-booking and payment software company Ingogo in May 2015.
This deal is ranked 8th on the List of highest funded equity crowdfunding projects.
This deal is ranked 8th on the List of highest funded equity crowdfunding projects.
In June 2016, VentureCrowd raised more than $900,000 for a Western Sydney residential project - a 35-lot development in Riverstone East, in partnership with the property developer ClearState.
In August 2016, a second project raised $1,700,000 for a 44-lot development project in Austral.
Brody Dalle (born Bree Joanna Alice Robinson; 1 January 1979) is an Australian-born singer-songwriter and guitarist.
Dalle began playing music in her adolescence, and moved to Los Angeles, California at age eighteen, where she found the punk rock band The Distillers.
The group released three albums before disbanding in 2006, and Dalle began another project, Spinnerette, releasing an eponymous album in 2009.
In 2014, she released "Diploid Love", her first album under her solo name.
Virginia HO/T lanes refers to five separate projects in the U.S. state of Virginia.
The first project, completed in November 2012, has added high-occupancy/toll (HO/T) lanes to the Capital Beltway (I-495) in Fairfax County.

The second project, opened to the public in December 2014, involved converting and extending the existing reversible high-occupancy vehicle (HOV) lanes on I-95 and I-395 to HO/T lanes from Stafford to near Alexandria.
The third project, currently under construction (as of December 2016), will convert the existing high-occupancy vehicle (HOV) lanes inside the beltway on Interstate 66 to HO/T lanes.
The fourth project will convert the existing high-occupancy vehicle (HOV) lane outside the beltway on Interstate 66 and add a second lane to HO/T lanes.
The fifth project will convert the existing reversible high-occupancy vehicle (HOV) lanes on I-395 to HO/T lanes from near Alexandria to Washington, DC.
The sixth project will convert the existing reversible HOV Lanes to HOT Lanes on Interstate 64 from Interstate 564 in Norfolk to Interstate 264 in Virginia Beach.
Catherine Hickson (born in 1955) is a Canadian volcanologist formerly part of the Geological Survey of Canada.
Hickson has mapped and studied young volcanoes in Canada assessing their past and future eruptions.
In 2003, Hickson was one of the three scientists to report on a hypothetical eruption at the Canadian volcano Mount Cayley.
Hickson works in South America where some of the largest and highest volcanoes are, including those in Argentina, Bolivia, Chile and Peru.
She was the creator and leader of the Multinational Andean Project.
The project ran from 1996 - 2002, with Argentina, Bolivia, Chile, and Peru, being co-funded by the Canadian International Development Agency and the participating countries.
In 2002 a second project was started that focused on volcanoes, earthquake and landslides and was expanded to include Ecuador, Colombia, and Venezuela.
It ended in 2006.

The work garnered Hickson the prestigious C.J. Westerman Memorial Award (2010) presented by the Association of Professional Engineers and Geoscientists of BC, and the Head of the Public Service Award (1999) among other awards.

In addition to her scientific contributions, she has made contributions to many publications for young people and the public, as well as being a sought after lecturer, field trip leader and supporter of science for the public.
She was an eyewitness to the 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens and wrote a book in 2005 describing her experience and explaining the mechanism of the eruption for the public.
She now works in the private sector.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What is the second project of the vocalist of the band The Distillers?
hop1_docs: The Distillers | The Distillers were an Australian-American punk rock band, formed in Los Angeles, California in 1998 by vocalist and guitarist Brody Dalle.  Dalle co-wrote, played guitar and provided vocals for nearly every track on the band's three albums.  After the breakup of the band in 2006, Dalle and Distillers guitarist Tony Bevilacqua went on to form Spinnerette.

Spinnerette | Spinnerette is an alternative rock band formed in 2007.  The band consists of Brody Dalle (the Distillers), Tony Bevilacqua (the Distillers), Jack Irons (What Is This? , Red Hot Chili Peppers, Walk the Moon, Eleven, Pearl Jam) and Alain Johannes (What Is This? , Walk the Moon, Eleven, Queens of the Stone Age, Them Crooked Vultures).  The band has been inactive since 2010 and its future is uncertain.

What Do I Do Now? | "What Do I Do Now?"  is a song by English Britpop band Sleeper, written by the band's vocalist and guitarist Louise Wener.  It was the first single to be released from their second album "The It Girl" in 1995 (see 1995 in British music).

Sound of Guns | Sound of Guns are an alternative rock band from Liverpool, England formed in 2008.  Discovered by "BBC Introducing" the band went on to sign with independent record label Distiller Records through which they released their debut album "What Came From Fire" on 28 June 2010 and second album "Angels and Enemies" on 5 March 2012.

Come What(ever) May | Come What(ever) May is the second studio album by American heavy metal band Stone Sour.  It was recorded and produced by the band and Nick Raskulinecz at Studio 606 in Los Angeles, California, and was released on August 1, 2006, by Roadrunner Records.  Writing for the album began as early as 2003 when vocalist Corey Taylor and guitarist James Root were writing material for their other band, Slipknot.  In January 2006, Stone Sour began recording their second album, during which time drummer Joel Ekman left the band because of family constraints.  He was eventually replaced by ex-Soulfly drummer Roy Mayorga who played on all but two tracks on the album.

Tony Martin (British singer) | Anthony Philip Harford (born 19 April 1957), better known by his stage name Tony Martin, is a heavy metal vocalist, best known for his time fronting Black Sabbath, initially from 1987 to 1991 and again from 1993 to 1997.  Martin was the band's second longest serving vocalist after Ozzy Osbourne.  He has since been involved in many other projects (such as M3, Misha Calvin, The Cage, Giuntini Project, and Phenomena).

That's What Friends Are For (Slade song) | That's What Friends Are For is a song by the British rock band Slade, released in 1987 as the second single from their fourteenth studio album "You Boyz Make Big Noize".  The song was written by lead vocalist Noddy Holder and bassist Jim Lea, and produced by Roy Thomas Baker.  It reached No. 95 in the UK, remaining in the charts for the one week.
hop1_titles: That's What Friends Are For (Slade song)
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- The Distillers — [1] "The Distillers were an Australian-American punk rock band,"
- Brody Dalle — [1] "vocalist and guitarist Brody Dalle."
- Spinnerette — [1] "went on to form Spinnerette." ; [2] "Spinnerette is an alternative rock band formed in 2007."
- Tony Bevilacqua — [1] "Dalle and Distillers guitarist Tony Bevilacqua went on to form Spinnerette." ; [2] "Tony Bevilacqua (the Distillers)"

Ambiguities:
- "second project" — passages do not state an ordinal; unclear whether "second project" means Spinnerette or another project.
- Brody Dalle's project list/order — not provided; possible other projects not shown.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing explicit chronological ordering of Brody Dalle's musical projects (i.e., confirmation that Spinnerette is her second project and what counts as a "project").

Retrieval direction:
- Retrieve page: "Brody Dalle" (full Wikipedia/biography) to get a chronology of her projects and confirm ordinal positions.
- Retrieve page: "Spinnerette" (already listed but re-check chronology and formation date).
- Search query: "Brody Dalle projects list" OR "Brody Dalle bands chronology" to find other projects and their dates.
- Optional: "The Distillers" page for timeline context.
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.
hop2_docs: Brody Dalle | Brody Dalle (born Bree Joanna Alice Robinson; 1 January 1979) is an Australian-born singer-songwriter and guitarist.  Dalle began playing music in her adolescence, and moved to Los Angeles, California at age eighteen, where she found the punk rock band The Distillers.  The group released three albums before disbanding in 2006, and Dalle began another project, Spinnerette, releasing an eponymous album in 2009.  In 2014, she released "Diploid Love", her first album under her solo name.

The Distillers | The Distillers were an Australian-American punk rock band, formed in Los Angeles, California in 1998 by vocalist and guitarist Brody Dalle.  Dalle co-wrote, played guitar and provided vocals for nearly every track on the band's three albums.  After the breakup of the band in 2006, Dalle and Distillers guitarist Tony Bevilacqua went on to form Spinnerette.

Nick Valensi | Nicholas "Nick" Valensi (born January 16, 1981) is an American guitarist, singer, songwriter and session musician.  He is most famous for his role as lead and rhythm guitarist, as well as occasional backing vocalist and mellotron player, in the American rock band The Strokes.  Valensi has also worked as a songwriter and session guitarist with various artists, including Sia, Blondie, Brody Dalle, Regina Spektor and Kate Pierson.  In 2013, he founded side-project CRX, for which he acts as singer, songwriter and lead and rhythm guitarist.  Their debut album, "New Skin", was released on October 28, 2016.

David Hidalgo Jr. | David Hidalgo Jr. (born August 30, 1984) is an American drummer currently playing in Social Distortion.  Hidalgo replaced former drummer Scott Reeder, who had been busy with his main project Fu Manchu.  Prior to joining Social Distortion, he played drums in The Drips and Suicidal Tendencies.  He also plays drums for The Bronx/Mariachi El Bronx and the hardcore punk band Bullet Treatment with a variety of lineups that also included members of Rise Against, The Bronx, Cancer Bats, Anti-Flag and others.  David also toured with Brody Dalle in her Spinnerette and self-titled projects.  He has recorded and toured with Chuck Ragan and Dave Hause.  Most recently, Dave recorded on Greg Graffin's new solo record.  He is the son of David Hidalgo, guitarist and singer of Los Lobos.

Cope (Freeland album) | Cope (stylized as COPE™) is the second album by English DJ and record producer Adam Freeland, credited only by his surname.  It was released on 8 June 2009 by Marine Parade Records.  Freeland worked with Alex Metric and collaborated with artists, such as Kurt Baumann, Brody Dalle, Gerald Casale, John Ceparano and Kim Field, who contributed vocals to songs on the album.

Diploid Love | Diploid Love is the debut solo album by Brody Dalle, former frontwoman of The Distillers and Spinnerette.  The digital download was released on April 28, 2014 in the United States and April 29 in Canada, followed by the CD and vinyl record set on May 19.  The lead single, "Meet the Foetus/Oh the Joy", was streamed for free on Soundcloud on February 9 before being released to iTunes on February 17.

Spinnerette | Spinnerette is an alternative rock band formed in 2007.  The band consists of Brody Dalle (the Distillers), Tony Bevilacqua (the Distillers), Jack Irons (What Is This? , Red Hot Chili Peppers, Walk the Moon, Eleven, Pearl Jam) and Alain Johannes (What Is This? , Walk the Moon, Eleven, Queens of the Stone Age, Them Crooked Vultures).  The band has been inactive since 2010 and its future is uncertain.
response.summary_2: Brody Dalle began the project Spinnerette after The Distillers disbanded, making Spinnerette her subsequent musical project.
